How Granite Construction stock looks after recent performance

Granite Construction (GVA) has attracted fresh investor attention after a period where its share price showed mixed short term moves but stronger longer term returns, prompting closer scrutiny of its current valuation and fundamentals.

The stock’s recent returns include about 0.9% over the past day, a small decline over the past week, and gains over the past month and past 3 months. Over longer horizons, total returns over the past year, 3 years, and 5 years are also positive, which may lead investors to compare that record with the company’s current earnings profile.

Granite Construction reports annual revenue of about US$4.4b and net income of roughly US$193.0m, with both revenue and net income showing annual growth on the latest figures provided. With a market value around US$5.3b, the company sits in the mid cap range for US contractors and materials providers.

The business operates through two primary segments: Construction and Materials, with Construction generating the majority of revenue. Materials, which includes aggregates, asphalt concrete, liquid asphalt, and recycled products, contributes a smaller share. There is also an elimination line that reflects internal transfers between the segments.

Granite Construction focuses on infrastructure solutions across roads, bridges, rail lines, airports, ports, dams, reservoirs, and water related projects, along with complex work such as tunnels, mining projects, and energy related builds including solar and battery storage. The Materials segment supports both internal projects and external customers, ranging from government agencies to private developers and industrial users.

data.title: Fiskars Oyj Abp (HLSE:FSKRS) Trailing 0.8% Net Margin Tests Bullish Recovery Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Fiskars Oyj Abp Q1 2026 Earnings Snapshot

Fiskars Oyj Abp (HLSE:FSKRS) has followed up a volatile 2025 with quarterly revenue ranging from €255.9 million to €337.2 million and basic EPS swinging between a loss of €0.16 and a profit of €0.45, while trailing twelve month figures sit at €1.14 billion of revenue and basic EPS of €0.12. Over the last few quarters, revenue has moved from €337.2 million in Q4 2024 to €291.9 million in Q1 2025 and then to €330.7 million by Q4 2025, with net income shifting from a €36.8 million profit to a €13.2 million loss and back to a €19.4 million profit as margins compressed against weak trailing profitability.

See our full analysis for Fiskars Oyj Abp.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings path lines up with the prevailing narratives about Fiskars's growth potential, margin risks and the balance between downside and upside in the story.

See what the community is saying about Fiskars Oyj Abp

Margins Feel Tight At 0.8% Net

  • On a trailing basis, Fiskars converted €1.14b of revenue into only €9.3 million of net income, which works out to a 0.8% net margin compared with 2.3% a year earlier. That 0.8% also includes a €4.8 million one off gain.
  • Consensus narrative points to cost work and channel mix as long term helpers for margins. However, the current 0.8% margin and heavy dependence on mature wholesale and U.S. retail channels keep the bearish concern about an inflexible cost base alive.
    • Critics highlight that process style businesses, like premium glassware, can see margins pressured when volumes soften. This lines up with the thin profit being earned on more than €1.1b of sales.
    • At the same time, the consensus view expects margins to move toward 7.2% over a few years. The gap between today’s 0.8% and that goal is exactly what bears focus on when they talk about execution risk.
On top of that thin margin, interest costs and the 6.19% dividend yield are flagged as stretch points for the balance sheet. This is central to the cautious, more bearish angle on Fiskars.🐻 Fiskars Oyj Abp Bear Case

P/E Of 117.6x Versus Richer Story

  • Fiskars trades on a trailing P/E of 117.6x compared with 31.1x for direct peers and 14.1x for the wider Consumer Durables group, even though trailing net income over the last twelve months was €9.3 million on €1.14b of revenue.
  • Supportive investors argue that expected earnings growth of about 25.2% a year and a DCF fair value of €16.39 versus a €13.56 share price justify paying more today, but the stretched P/E gives bears plenty to point at.
    • Consensus narrative talks about growth from new categories, direct to consumer channels and emerging markets. Yet the current margin and small profit base mean a lot has to go right for that 25.2% growth path to translate into materially higher earnings.
    • What stands out is the tug of war between a DCF suggesting the share price is roughly 17.2% below DCF fair value and a P/E that sits several times above both peers and the broader industry. Investors will likely weigh this against their own expectations.

Dividend Yield 6.19% With Weak Coverage

  • The stock offers a 6.19% dividend yield, but trailing earnings and free cash flow are not enough to fully cover that payout. Interest coverage is also described as weak on recent numbers.
  • Consensus narrative often leans on premium brands and a stronger direct to consumer mix as long term supports for cash generation. Yet today’s combination of thin 0.8% margins, modest forecast revenue growth of 2.9% a year and elevated net debt metrics keeps the cautious view on cash flow firmly in play.
    • Bears argue that higher inventories and softer volumes have already pushed net debt to earnings measures above the company’s own comfort zone, which sits awkwardly alongside the current high dividend commitment.
    • On the other hand, analysts expect earnings to reach €89.8 million by around 2028, so anyone leaning bullish will be testing whether that forecast is realistic enough to eventually support both the dividend and debt service from internal cash.
Supporters who want to stress test those growth and margin assumptions against detailed earnings paths often start by lining them up with the full narrative around Fiskars’s future, then compare that to how other investors frame the risks.🐂 Fiskars Oyj Abp Bull Case

Next Steps

Given the mix of optimism and caution running through this story, it makes sense to move quickly, look through the numbers yourself and decide what feels realistic for your goals, then weigh those impressions against the 2 key rewards and 4 important warning signs

data.description:

Approach 1: Albemarle Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, using a required rate of return. It focuses on the cash that might be available to shareholders rather than accounting earnings.

For Albemarle, the model starts with last twelve months Free Cash Flow of about $152.2 million. Analysts have provided cash flow estimates out to 2028, including projected Free Cash Flow of $479.4 million in that year, with further annual figures out to 2035 extrapolated by Simply Wall St using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. All projections are in $.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back and combined with an assumed value beyond the explicit forecast period, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $34.29 per share. Compared to a recent share price around $193, this implies the stock is very expensive on this model and is described as 463.2% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Albemarle Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings can be volatile or negative, the P/S ratio is often a useful cross check because it compares the share price to the revenue the business generates, rather than accounting profit. It still reflects how the market weighs growth expectations and risk, since investors usually accept a higher P/S when they expect stronger sales growth or see lower risk in those sales.

Albemarle currently trades on a P/S of 4.43x. This sits above both the Chemicals industry average of 1.09x and the peer group average of 2.83x. This suggests investors are paying a higher price for each dollar of Albemarle's revenue than for many competitors.

Simply Wall St's Fair Ratio for Albemarle, at 1.74x, is its estimate of what a reasonable P/S might be given factors like the company's earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. Since Albemarle's actual P/S of 4.43x is well above the Fair Ratio of 1.74x, the shares look expensive on this metric.

data.title: Simona (DB:SIM0) Margin Squeeze And EPS Slide Challenge Bullish Earnings Recovery Narratives
data.description:
SIMONA (DB:SIM0) has reported its FY 2025 first half with revenue of €305.8 million and basic EPS of €1.08, setting the tone against a trailing twelve month backdrop of €590.0 million in revenue and EPS of €3.18. Over recent periods, the company has seen revenue move from €301.3 million in 1H 2024 to €283.7 million in 2H 2024 and then to €305.8 million in 1H 2025. Basic EPS shifted from €2.21 to €1.62 and then to €1.08. This pattern keeps the focus squarely on how profit margins are holding up through this latest reporting stretch.

See our full analysis for SIMONA.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing growth and risk narratives that investors have been watching over the past year.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

3.2% net margin puts profitability in context

  • Over the last 12 months, SIMONA produced €590.0 million in revenue and €19.1 million in net income, which works out to a 3.2% net margin compared with 3.9% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a more cautious, bearish view is that this thinner 3.2% margin sits alongside five year earnings that declined by about 10.6% per year, which can make the recent drop from €13.2 million net income in 1H 2024 to €6.5 million in 1H 2025 look like part of a longer profit squeeze rather than a one off pause.
    • Bears point to that sequence of half year net income figures, €13.2 million, then €9.7 million, then €6.5 million, as evidence that profitability has been under pressure across multiple periods, not just this latest half.
    • The move in basic EPS over the same halves, from €2.21 to €1.62 to €1.08, lines up with that concern and gives the bearish side firm numbers to reference when they question how quickly margins can rebuild.

P/E of 16.8x versus peers and DCF fair value

  • The shares trade on a P/E of 16.8x at a price of €53.50, below the wider European Chemicals industry at 19.1x but above the peer group average of 10.3x, while a DCF fair value of €107.28 sits well above the current share price.
  • Supporters with a more bullish tilt argue that this mix, a P/E lower than the broader industry and a DCF fair value nearly twice the current €53.50 level, suggests the market is not fully pricing in the forecast 27% annual earnings growth, even though the premium to the 10.3x peer average keeps the debate open.
    • The projected 27% yearly earnings growth compared with the German market’s 16.0% gives bulls a clear growth angle to set against the current valuation multiples.
    • At the same time, the modest 4.3% expected annual revenue growth and the lower 3.2% trailing margin both pull in the other direction, so the bullish case rests heavily on profit per euro of sales improving from here rather than just on sales volume.

Revenue trend steady while growth forecasts diverge

  • On the top line, revenue across the last three halves has moved from €301.3 million in 1H 2024 to €283.7 million in 2H 2024 and then €305.8 million in 1H 2025, while forward looking estimates point to about 4.3% yearly revenue growth but around 27% yearly earnings growth.
  • What is interesting for readers comparing views is how this combination of relatively steady revenue and much stronger earnings forecasts contrasts with the recent five year earnings decline of about 10.6% per year, putting the focus on whether the business can convert similar revenue levels into higher profit without a big sales jump.
    • The trailing twelve month revenue line, sitting close together at €581.9 million, €586.4 million and €590.0 million over the last three data points, highlights that forecasts of faster earnings growth are not currently backed by a rapid sales ramp.
    • Alongside that, the forecast 4.3% revenue growth running slower than the 6.1% expected for the German market explains why some investors pay close attention to cost control and margin discipline rather than assuming that higher sales alone will carry future profit.

data.title: We Think That There Are Some Issues For Waaree Renewable Technologies (NSE:WAAREERTL) Beyond Its Promising Earnings
data.description:
Following the solid earnings report from Waaree Renewable Technologies Limited (NSE:WAAREERTL), the market responded by bidding up the stock price. Despite this, our analysis suggests that there are some factors weakening the foundations of those good profit numbers.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Waaree Renewable Technologies' Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Over the twelve months to March 2026, Waaree Renewable Technologies recorded an accrual ratio of 0.64. That means it didn't generate anywhere near enough free cash flow to match its profit. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. Indeed, in the last twelve months it reported free cash flow of ₹1.6b, which is significantly less than its profit of ₹4.79b. Waaree Renewable Technologies' free cash flow actually declined over the last year, but it may bounce back next year, since free cash flow is often more volatile than accounting profits.

Our Take On Waaree Renewable Technologies' Profit Performance

For this reason, we think that Waaree Renewable Technologies' statutory profits may be a bad guide to its underlying earnings power, and might give investors an overly positive impression of the company. But on the bright side, its earnings per share have grown at an extremely impressive rate over the last three years. Of course, we've only just scratched the surface when it comes to analysing its earnings; one could also consider margins, forecast growth, and return on investment, among other factors. data.title: Why You Might Be Interested In Macbee Planet, Inc. (TSE:7095) For Its Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Macbee Planet, Inc. (TSE:7095) stock is about to trade ex-dividend in three days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is an important date to be aware of as any purchase of the stock made on or after this date might mean a late settlement that doesn't show on the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Macbee Planet's shares before the 28th of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 31st of July.

The company's next dividend payment will be JP¥55.00 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed JP¥55.00 to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Macbee Planet has a trailing yield of 4.1% on the current share price of JP¥1340.00. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. So we need to investigate whether Macbee Planet can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Macbee Planet paid out just 14% of its profit last year, which we think is conservatively low and leaves plenty of margin for unexpected circumstances. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Macbee Planet generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. The good news is it paid out just 24% of its free cash flow in the last year.

It's positive to see that Macbee Planet's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

See our latest analysis for Macbee Planet

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Businesses with strong growth prospects usually make the best dividend payers, because it's easier to grow dividends when earnings per share are improving. If earnings fall far enough, the company could be forced to cut its dividend. That's why it's comforting to see Macbee Planet's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 25% per annum for the past five years. Macbee Planet earnings per share have been sprinting ahead like the Road Runner at a track and field day; scarcely stopping even for a cheeky "beep-beep". We also like that it is reinvesting most of its profits in its business.'

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. Since the start of our data, two years ago, Macbee Planet has lifted its dividend by approximately 40% a year on average. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

Final Takeaway

Has Macbee Planet got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? We love that Macbee Planet is growing earnings per share while simultaneously paying out a low percentage of both its earnings and cash flow. These characteristics suggest the company is reinvesting in growing its business, while the conservative payout ratio also implies a reduced risk of the dividend being cut in the future. Macbee Planet looks solid on this analysis overall, and we'd definitely consider investigating it more closely.

data.title: Detection Technology Oyj Margins Slip To 6.4% Testing Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Q1 2026 snapshot for Detection Technology Oyj (HLSE:DETEC)

Detection Technology Oyj (HLSE:DETEC) opened 2026 with Q1 results that build on its recent track record, after posting Q4 2025 revenue of €27.2 million and basic EPS of €0.163 on net income of €2.39 million. Over the past few quarters, revenue has moved from €23.31 million in Q1 2025 to €24.37 million in Q2 and €27.2 million in both Q3 and Q4. Basic EPS shifted from €0.057 to €0.067 and then €0.163, giving you a clear view of how the top and bottom lines have scaled together. Against this backdrop, the key question for investors is how these earnings quality signals feed through to margins and whether that profitability profile looks durable.

See our full analysis for Detection Technology Oyj.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the dominant market narratives around Detection Technology Oyj's growth potential and risk profile.

See what the community is saying about Detection Technology Oyj

Margins Under Pressure At 6.4%

  • On a trailing 12 month view, Detection Technology Oyj is running at a 6.4% net profit margin, compared with 10.3% a year earlier, even though revenue over that period sits at about €101.0 million.
  • Critics in the bearish camp focus on exactly this kind of margin squeeze, arguing that price competition, customer concentration and cost cuts could keep profitability under strain even if volumes hold up.
    • The drop in net margin from 10.3% to 6.4% lines up with concerns about compressed average selling prices in larger markets like China and the impact of cost reduction efforts on operating leverage.
    • At the same time, trailing 12 month net income of around €6.4 million shows the business is still generating earnings, which challenges the most pessimistic idea that hardware focused detector suppliers are already being fully pushed aside by software heavy imaging solutions.
Bears warn that shrinking margins may keep a lid on future profit quality even if revenue grows, so it is worth seeing how that risk is framed in their full argument 🐻 Detection Technology Oyj Bear Case.

P/E Of 21.6x Versus Richer Peers

  • The trailing P/E sits at 21.6x, which is below the peer average of 39.9x and also below the wider European Electronic industry at 23.1x, while the current share price is €9.50 against a DCF fair value reference of about €19.01.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this valuation gap and argue the market is underestimating what faster earnings growth and expanding margins could mean for longer term returns.
    • Analysts are forecasting earnings growth of around 18.1% per year, ahead of the Finnish market forecast of 12.9%, which heavily supports the bullish claim that a 21.6x P/E multiple is not excessive given that growth profile.
    • With revenue forecast to grow by roughly 7.4% per year versus 4.2% for the Finnish market, the upside case sees current pricing and the roughly 50% discount to the €19.01 DCF fair value as a mismatch between the company’s growth outlook and how it is being priced today.
Bulls argue that a below peer P/E and a DCF fair value almost double the share price point to a mispriced growth story that is laid out in more depth in their detailed thesis 🐂 Detection Technology Oyj Bull Case.

Forecast Growth Versus Dividend Cash Strain

  • Forecasts call for revenue growth of about 7.4% per year and earnings growth of roughly 18.1% per year, while the trailing dividend yield is 3.16% and that dividend is not well covered by free cash flows.
  • The balanced consensus narrative sees this mix as a trade off, where growth potential and sector tailwinds sit alongside very real cash flow and payout pressures.
    • On one hand, expectations for higher earnings growth than the broader Finnish market and recovery in key sectors like healthcare imaging and security screening give some backing to the idea of a growing earnings base over time.
    • On the other hand, weak free cash flow coverage of a 3.16% dividend means that even if earnings expand, investors still need to watch how much of that shows up as dependable, cash backed shareholder returns rather than just accounting profit.
